source_text: “Come!” said Defarge. “Here is monsieur, who knows a well-made shoe when he sees one. Show him that shoe you are working at. Take it, monsieur.”
raw_output: “Come,” said Defarge. “Here is monsieur, who knows a well-made shoe when he sees one. Show him that shoe you are working on. Take it, monsieur.”
rewrite_text: “Come,” said Defarge. “Here is monsieur, who knows a well-made shoe when he sees one. Show him that shoe you are working on. Take it, monsieur.”

source_text: “I am the Resurrection and the Life, saith the Lord: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live: and whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die.”
raw_output: “I am the Resurrection and the Life, saith the Lord: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live; and whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die.”
rewrite_text: “I am the Resurrection and the Life, saith the Lord: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live; and whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die.”

source_text: “To the eye it is fair enough, here; but seen in its integrity, under the sky, and by the daylight, it is a crumbling tower of waste, mismanagement, extortion, debt, mortgage, oppression, hunger, nakedness, and suffering.”
raw_output: “To the eye it is fair enough here; but seen in its entirety, under the open sky and by daylight, it is a crumbling tower of waste, mismanagement, extortion, debt, mortgage, oppression, hunger, nakedness, and suffering.”
rewrite_text: “To the eye it is fair enough here; but seen in its entirety, under the open sky and by daylight, it is a crumbling tower of waste, mismanagement, extortion, debt, mortgage, oppression, hunger, nakedness, and suffering.”

source_text:
Arise, arise, arise!
There is blood on the earth that denies ye bread;
Be your wounds like eyes
To weep for the dead, the dead, the dead.
What other grief were it just to pay?
Your sons, your wives, your brethren, were they;
Who said they were slain on the battle day?
raw_output:
Arise, arise, arise!
There is blood on the earth that denies ye bread;
Let your wounds be as eyes
To weep for the dead, the dead, the dead.
What other grief were it just to pay?
Your sons, your wives, your brethren, were they;
Who said they were slain on the battle day?
rewrite_text:
Arise, arise, arise!
There is blood on the earth that denies ye bread;
Let your wounds be as eyes
To weep for the dead, the dead, the dead.
What other grief were it just to pay?
Your sons, your wives, your brethren, were they;
Who said they were slain on the battle day?

Conditional Text Generation with VAE. Conditional text generation has drawn much attention recently. By controlling the properties of generated contents, we can apply the generative models to many real-world scenarios. We follow the problem setting in (Hu et al., 2017) . Given a set of k conditions C = {c 1 , c 2 , ..., c k }, an unlabeled corpus X, and conditional text samples

source_text: We can improve robustness to noise at test time by introducing various forms of artificial noise during training. We distinct regularization methods like dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014) and task-specific data augmentation that transforms the data to resemble noisy input. The latter technique was successfully applied in other domains, including computer vision (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) and speech recognition (Sperber et al., 2017) . During training, we artificially induce noise into the original sentences using the algorithm described in §3.2 and train our models using a mixture of clean and noisy sentences. Let L 0 (x, y; θ) be the standard training objective for the sequence labeling problem, where x is the input sentence, y is the corresponding ground-truth sequence of labels, and θ represents the parameters of F (x). We define our composite loss function as follows:
raw_output: We can enhance robustness to test-time noise by injecting different types of artificial noise during training. We distinguish regularization methods such as dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014) from task-specific data augmentation, which modifies the data so that it more closely resembles noisy input. The latter approach has been successfully used in other areas, including computer vision (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) and speech recognition (Sperber et al., 2017). During training, we artificially add noise to the original sentences using the algorithm described in §3.2 and train our models on a mixture of clean and noisy sentences. Let L 0 (x, y; θ) denote the standard training objective for the sequence labeling task, where x is the input sentence, y is the corresponding gold label sequence, and θ denotes the parameters of F (x). We define our composite loss function as follows:

The latter approach has been successfully used in other areas, including computer vision (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) and speech recognition (Sperber et al., 2017). During training, we artificially add noise to the original sentences using the algorithm described in §3.2 and train our models on a mixture of clean and noisy sentences. source_text: The LSTM dual encoder model consists of two single-layer, uni-directional encoders, one to encode the embedding (c) of the context and one to encode the embedding (r) of the response. The output function is computed as a dot product of the two, f (r, c) = c T r. This model architecture has already been shown to perform well for the Ubuntu dataset (Lowe et al., 2015; Kadlec et al., 2015) . We carry out experiments with the following variants of the vanilla model for training:
raw_output: The LSTM dual encoder model comprises two single-layer, unidirectional encoders: one encodes the context embedding (c), and the other encodes the response embedding (r). The output function is calculated as the dot product of the two, f(r, c) = c^T r. This architectural design has already been demonstrated to work well on the Ubuntu dataset (Lowe et al., 2015; Kadlec et al., 2015). We conduct experiments using the following training variants of the vanilla model:
rewrite_text: The LSTM dual encoder model comprises two single-layer, unidirectional encoders: one encodes the context embedding (c), and the other encodes the response embedding (r). The output function is calculated as the dot product of the two, f(r, c) = c^T r. This architectural design has already been demonstrated to work well on the Ubuntu dataset (Lowe et al., 2015; Kadlec et al., 2015). source_text: The key idea behind an unreferenced dialogue metric is the use of Noise Contrastive Estimation (NCE) (Gutmann and Hyvärinen, 2010) for training. Specifically, we train the model to differentiate between a correct response (score(c, r) → 1), and a negative response (score(c,r) → 0), wherer represents a candidate false response for the given context c. The loss to minimize contains one positive example and a range of negative examples chosen from a sampling policy P (r):
raw_output: The central idea of an unreferenced dialogue metric is to employ Noise Contrastive Estimation (NCE) (Gutmann and Hyvärinen, 2010) for training. In particular, the model is trained to distinguish a correct response, for which score(c, r) → 1, from a negative response, for which score(c, r) → 0, where r denotes a candidate incorrect response for the given context c. The objective to be minimized includes one positive example together with a set of negative examples selected according to a sampling policy P(r):
rewrite_text: The central idea of an unreferenced dialogue metric is to employ Noise Contrastive Estimation (NCE) (Gutmann and Hyvärinen, 2010) for training. In particular, the model is trained to distinguish a correct response, for which score(c, r) → 1, from a negative response, for which score(c, r) → 0, where r denotes a candidate incorrect response for the given context c. The objective to be minimized includes one positive example together with a set of negative examples selected according to a sampling policy P(r):
